content:
# Acts Chapter 19 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 248-250 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_019_page_001.jpg, ACT_019_page_002.jpg, ACT_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And it came-to-pass while Apollos was being in Corinth, Paul having-passed-through the upper regions came to Ephesus, and found certain disciples,

**2.** and said to them, "Did you receive [the] Holy Spirit having-believed?" And they [said] to him, "But not even whether [the] Holy Spirit is have we heard."

**3.** And he said, "Into what then were you baptized?" And they said, "Into the baptism of John."

**4.** And Paul said, "John baptized [with] a baptism of repentance, saying to the people that into the [one] coming after him they should believe, that is, into Jesus."

**5.** And having-heard [this] they were baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.

**6.** And Paul having-laid [his] hands upon them, the Holy Spirit came upon them, and they were speaking in tongues and prophesying.

**7.** And they were, all [the] men, about twelve.

**8.** And having-entered into the synagogue he was speaking-boldly for three months, reasoning and persuading [them] concerning the kingdom of God.

**9.** But when some were being-hardened and were being-disobedient, speaking-evil-of the Way before the multitude, having-withdrawn from them he separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the lecture-hall of Tyrannus.

**10.** And this happened for two years, so-that all those dwelling in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.

**11.** And powers not-the-ordinary God was doing through the hands of Paul,

**12.** so-that even upon the sick were being-carried-away from his skin handkerchiefs or aprons, and the diseases were being-released from them, and the evil spirits were going-out.

**13.** And some of the wandering Jews, exorcists, attempted to name over those having the evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, "I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims."

**14.** And there were certain [men], sons of Sceva, a Jewish chief-priest, seven [sons], doing this.

**15.** And the evil spirit answering said to them, "Jesus I know, and Paul I know-about, but who are you?"

**16.** And the man in whom the evil spirit was, having-leaped upon them, having-overpowered both [of them], prevailed against them, so-that naked and having-been-wounded they fled out of that house.

**17.** And this became known to all, both Jews and Greeks, those dwelling in Ephesus, and fear fell upon all of them, and the name of the Lord Jesus was being-magnified.

**18.** And many of those having-believed were coming, confessing and declaring their deeds.

**19.** And a considerable-number of those having-practiced the magic-arts, having-brought-together the books, were burning [them] before all. And they counted-up the prices of them and found [it to be] fifty thousand [pieces] of silver.

**20.** Thus according-to [the] might of the Lord the word was growing and was prevailing.

**21.** And when these things were fulfilled, Paul purposed in the Spirit, having-passed-through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying that, "After I have been there, it is necessary [for] me also to see Rome."

**22.** And having-sent into Macedonia two of those ministering to him, Timothy and Erastus, he himself held-on for a time in Asia.

**23.** And there arose at that time no small disturbance concerning the Way.

**24.** For a certain-one, Demetrius by name, a silversmith, making silver shrines of Artemis, was providing no small business to the craftsmen,

**25.** whom having-gathered-together, and the workers concerning such things, he said, "Men, you know that from this business is our prosperity.

**26.** And you see and hear that not only [in] Ephesus but almost [in] all of Asia, this Paul having-persuaded [them] turned-away a considerable crowd, saying that they are not gods, those being-made by hands.

**27.** And not only this [is] in-danger for us, the part to come into disrepute, but also the temple of the great goddess Artemis to be reckoned as nothing, and also to be about to be pulled-down [from] her majesty, whom all Asia and the inhabited-world worships."

**28.** And having-heard [this] and having-become full of anger, they were crying-out saying, "Great [is] Artemis of [the] Ephesians!"

**29.** And the city was filled with the confusion, and they rushed with-one-accord into the theater, having-seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, traveling-companions of Paul.

**30.** And Paul wanting to enter into the populace, the disciples were not allowing him.

**31.** And some also of the Asiarchs, being friends to him, having-sent to him, were urging [him] not to give himself into the theater.

**32.** Others then were crying-out some other-thing, for the assembly was having-been-confused, and the majority did not know for what reason they had come-together.

**33.** And from the crowd they put-forward Alexander, the Jews having-pushed him forward. And Alexander, having-motioned [with] the hand, was wanting to make-a-defense to the populace.

**34.** But having-recognized that he is a Jew, one voice came from all, crying-out for about two hours, "Great [is] Artemis of [the] Ephesians!"

**35.** And the town-clerk having-quieted the crowd says, "Men, Ephesians, for who is [there] of men who does not know the city of [the] Ephesians being temple-keeper of the great Artemis and of the [image] fallen-from-Zeus?

**36.** Therefore these-things being undeniable, it is necessary [for] you to be having-been-quieted and to do nothing reckless.

**37.** For you brought these men, neither temple-robbers nor blaspheming our goddess.

**38.** If then Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a matter against anyone, court-days are being-held and there are proconsuls; let them bring-charges against one-another.

**39.** But if you seek anything concerning other matters, in the lawful assembly it shall be resolved.

**40.** For indeed we are in-danger of being-accused of insurrection concerning today, there being no cause concerning which we shall be able to give an account of this disorderly-gathering."

**41.** And having-said these-things, he dismissed the assembly.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 2:** Sinaiticus reads "Did you receive the Holy Spirit having-believed?" (ei pneuma hagion elabete pisteusantes). The question about the Holy Spirit is direct, with the aorist participle pisteusantes indicating action contemporaneous with or prior to receiving.

2. **Verse 5:** Sinaiticus reads "into the name of the Lord Jesus" (eis to onoma tou kyriou Iesou), rather than "of the Lord Jesus Christ" found in some later manuscripts. The shorter form is characteristic of Sinaiticus.

3. **Verse 9:** Sinaiticus reads "lecture-hall of Tyrannus" (schole Tyrannou). The Western text (Codex D) adds "from the fifth hour to the tenth," a detail absent from Sinaiticus. This famous addition specifies Paul taught during the hottest hours when the hall was available, but it is not original to this manuscript.

4. **Verse 12:** Sinaiticus reads "from his skin" (apo tou chromatos autou) for "from his body," using chromatos (skin/surface) rather than the more common somatos (body) found in some traditions.

5. **Verse 14:** Sinaiticus reads "seven sons" of Sceva. Sceva is called archiereos (chief-priest). Whether he was literally a high priest or used the title loosely remains debated. Sinaiticus preserves archiereos without qualification.

6. **Verse 16:** Sinaiticus reads "having-overpowered both" (katakyrieusas amphoteron), using the dual "both" which is notable since verse 14 mentions seven sons. This may indicate that only two of the seven were present at this particular encounter. Some manuscripts read "all" (panton) instead.

7. **Verse 19:** The sum "fifty thousand of silver" (arguriou muriadas pente) -- fifty thousand silver drachmas, an enormous sum equivalent to roughly 137 years of daily wages for a common laborer.

8. **Verse 20:** Sinaiticus reads "according to the might of the Lord" (kata kratos tou kyriou), attributing the growth of the word to the Lord's power. This is the reading of the major uncials.

9. **Verse 27:** Sinaiticus includes the extended warning about Artemis's temple being "reckoned as nothing" and her majesty "about to be pulled down." The grammar is complex, with multiple infinitive constructions dependent on kindyneuei (is in danger).

10. **Verse 33:** The role of Alexander is ambiguous in Sinaiticus as in all manuscripts. Whether the Jews pushed him forward to distance themselves from the riot, or to speak on their behalf, the text does not clarify. The verb probebasanton (having-pushed-forward) suggests initiative from the Jewish community.

11. **Verse 35:** Sinaiticus reads "the [image] fallen-from-Zeus" (tou diopetous), referring to the cult image of Artemis believed to have fallen from heaven. The adjective diopetous (Zeus-fallen) is used substantivally here.

12. **Verse 40:** Sinaiticus preserves the town-clerk's warning about legal liability for the riot (staseos), a term with serious political implications under Roman law. The phrase "no cause" (meden aition) emphasizes the absence of legitimate grounds for the assembly.

-- EMET (Truth)
